---
title: "Architecture Boundaries"
---

# Architecture Boundaries

These are the rules that keep the codebase clean and both runtimes maintainable. Violating them creates coupling between the application layer and a specific runtime, which defeats the purpose of the storage and transport seams.

Do not violate these boundaries.

## Components and stores call repositories only

Application code (Svelte components, SvelteKit routes, stores) must interact with data exclusively through the repository layer in `src/lib/db/`. They must never import the `db` object or drizzle query builders directly.

The drizzle `db` object is private to `src/lib/db/`. It is exposed to the rest of the app through `getDb()` and the repository functions in `src/lib/db/repos/`.

This rule exists because the `db` object is tied to a specific driver. If a component imported it directly, that component would be coupled to whichever driver is active at import time, making it impossible to test against the in-memory driver or to swap runtimes.

## StorageDriver is the single storage seam

`StorageDriver` (`src/lib/db/driver/types.ts`) is the interface that all storage backends implement. It has three core methods:

- `query(sql, params?)` — run a statement that returns rows (SELECT).
- `batch(stmts)` — run many statements in one round-trip.
- `exec(sql)` — run a statement that returns no rows (DDL, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE).

Three concrete implementations satisfy this interface:

| Driver | Runtime | Description |
|---|---|---|
| OPFS Worker | Browser | SQLite-WASM + Origin Private File System. Runs in a Web Worker; communicates with the main thread over `postMessage`. |
| Tauri SQL plugin | Desktop | Native SQLite via `@tauri-apps/plugin-sql`. |
| In-memory sql.js | Tests | Ephemeral SQLite for Vitest. |

Drizzle, the schema, and the repository layer live on the main thread. Drivers are dumb SQL executors — the OPFS worker literally just runs SQL strings and returns row data.

## Runtime selection

Runtime selection happens in `src/lib/db/driver/client.ts` via the `isTauri()` guard from `@tauri-apps/api/core`. If the code is running inside a Tauri webview, the Tauri SQL plugin driver is instantiated. Otherwise, the OPFS worker driver is used.

This is the only place in the codebase where runtime detection occurs. All code above the driver layer is runtime-agnostic.

## No secrets in settings

The `settings` table is a key/value store for non-secret configuration. Provider configuration (base URL, default model, template type, display name) lives in `settings` as JSON values.

API keys are **never** stored in `settings`. They are stored in:

- **Desktop:** the OS keychain via the Rust `keyring` crate. The plaintext key never enters the webview JavaScript context. The Tauri bridge exposes only `key_has` (boolean), `key_set`, and `key_delete` commands.
- **Browser:** IndexedDB under the same origin as the app.

A one-time `migrateLegacyKeys()` boot step moves any legacy `providerKey:<id>` rows out of `settings` into the runtime key store. This handles upgrades from pre-P5 builds.

The `ProviderConfig` type in the codebase intentionally omits any key field. The settings UI stores a key, then immediately masks the input with a "Replace key" affordance. The key is never echoed back after save.
